text: I.	Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights

children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation – Mandates and background

24.1	The United Nations human rights programme is responsible for the promotion, protection, enjoyment and full realization, by all people, of all human rights. The mandate derives from the provisions of the Charter of the United Nations; the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and international human rights treaties; the Vienna Declaration and Programme of Action, including its principles and recommendations subsequently endorsed by the General Assembly in its resolution 48/121; resolution 48/141, by which the Assembly established the post of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights; the outcomes of relevant United Nations conferences and summits, including resolution 70/1, entitled “Transforming our world: the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development”; the Pact for the Future (resolution 79/1); and other relevant Assembly and Human Rights Council resolutions and decisions. In addition to international human rights law, the programme is also guided by international humanitarian law, as applicable.

24.2	The programme is guided by the principles of universality, objectivity, impartiality, indivisibility and non-selectivity in removing obstacles to the full realization of all human rights and in preventing the continuation of human rights violations, including in cooperation with relevant stakeholders. The programme has a role in supporting the implementation by Member States of the 2030 Agenda, making development equitable, sustainable and responsive to the needs of people, including in relation to conflict prevention and resolution.

24.3	The programme falls under the leadership of the High Commissioner for Human Rights who, pursuant to General Assembly resolution 48/141, is entrusted with the principal responsibility for the human rights activities of the Organization, under the direction and authority of the Secretary-General and within the framework of the overall competence, authority and decisions of the Assembly and the Human Rights Council.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

24.4	In a global context affected by threats to the enjoyment of human rights by all, the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights (OHCHR) will continue to support the human rights mechanisms and to assist States in their efforts to comply with their human rights obligations. Increased support and cooperation in matters regarding human rights will be provided to requesting Member States through mutually agreed bilateral frameworks. Emphasis will be placed on highlighting the importance of human rights in international and national agendas; sustaining social development and social justice (as defined in General Assembly resolution 62/10) and countering discrimination on all grounds; advancing the rights of children and women; raising awareness of human rights; responding to the needs of the vulnerable for protection; and addressing situations of international concern as identified by the Assembly, the Human Rights Council and other competent intergovernmental organs.

24.5	The Office will continue to engage with Member States and all relevant actors to assist them in their efforts to promote and protect human rights, through the provision of technical assistance, support for human rights bodies and mechanisms and, where appropriate, the establishment or strengthening of regional or country presences. Consolidated organizational support will be provided to the Human Rights Council and its subsidiary bodies and mechanisms, the universal periodic review, the special procedures, the Human Rights Council Advisory Committee and the complaint procedure, as well as other relevant United Nations bodies. The United Nations treaty bodies will continue to be provided with services, support and advice.

24.6	It is proposed that the work of the Office of the Victims’ Rights Advocate (previously under section 1 of the proposed programme budget) be incorporated into the programme of work of OHCHR, specifically subprogramme 3, Advisory services, technical cooperation and field activities. Through the Victims’ Rights Advocate, the Office will ensure that the rights and dignity of victims of sexual exploitation and abuse are prioritized in all efforts of the United Nations system to prevent and respond to sexual exploitation and abuse by its personnel, uniformed or civilian, in peacekeeping, humanitarian and development settings and by non-United Nations personnel serving under a Security Council mandate.

24.7	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, the Office will use multi-stakeholder partnerships to support countries in the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals and its transformative promise of leaving no one behind. The Office will continue to engage with a wide range of stakeholders, including civil society actors, such as non-governmental organizations (NGOs). It will also continue to help victims of human rights violations by managing the humanitarian funds that provide financial assistance for support services, such as rehabilitation and redress for victims of torture and slavery.

24.8	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Office will help to ensure that human rights considerations are systematically integrated into United Nations policies and activities, including through the United Nations Human Rights Group, a system-wide internal coordination mechanism, led by the High Commissioner for Human Rights. The Office will continue to engage with the United Nations development system, both through participation in inter-agency forums and cooperation with United Nations country teams on the ground. The Office will continue to cooperate with missions with a human rights component supported by the Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs and the Department of Peace Operations. It will also continue its collaboration with all the United Nations agencies and programmes in crisis situations, such as humanitarian emergencies.
24.9	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	There is political will on the part of States to work with the programme;
(b)	Extrabudgetary resources will continue to be provided for the programme.

24.10	The programme integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. For example, subprogramme 2 will deliver capacity-building activities on the follow-up to treaty body recommendations, including those related to the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination against Women.

24.11	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Office will integrate disability inclusion into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. For example, subprogramme 1 will provide expert advice on the development of standards on strengthening national human rights promotion and protection capacities as they relate to persons with disabilities.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

24.12	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated planned deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026. In addition, the Office will enhance regional engagement to increase impact at the regional and country levels. Strengthened engagement at the regional level will support better integration across all United Nations activities and facilitate capacity-building and technical assistance tailored to Member States’ requests and needs. During the course of 2026, the United Nations Human Rights Group, a system-wide internal coordination mechanism, established by the Secretary-General, will be operationalized. The Group, comprising entities from across the United Nations system, led by the High Commissioner for Human Rights, will ensure that human rights considerations are systematically integrated into United Nations policies and activities, while also improving efficiency and impact across the system at all levels. In addition , as outlined in paragraph 24.6 above, it is proposed that the Office of the Victims’ Rights Advocate (previously under section 1 of the proposed programme budget) be incorporated into the programme of work of OHCHR, as reflected in the updated strategy, expected results and deliverables of subprogramme 3, Advisory services, technical cooperation and field activities.

24.13	The Office will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs.

children.text: Evaluation activities
24.16	The following evaluations, completed in 2025, have guided the proposed programme plan for 2027:
(a)	Office of Internal Oversight Services evaluation of the Office;
(b)	Evaluation conducted by the Office on its Guatemala country programme;

(c)	Three project evaluations conducted by the Office on human rights compliance in law enforcement in the Sahel, responses to human rights violations related to irregular migration in West Africa and the protection of specific human rights concerns in Latin America and the Caribbean.

24.17	In response to the results of the evaluations referenced above, several programme strategies, theories of change and planning frameworks have been updated or developed. For example, in relation to the project evaluations referenced above, guidance and resources supporting the design of results frameworks, as well as results-based reporting, have been developed and disseminated, including new guidance notes on theory of change development and the formulation of output indicators. In response to the results of the Office of Internal Oversight Services evaluation of the Office, programmatic capacity at the regional and country levels are being strengthened.
24.18	The following evaluations, to be conducted by the Office, are planned for 2027:
(a)	One cross-cutting thematic evaluation;
(b)	Three programme/project evaluations.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Human rights mainstreaming, right to development, and research and analysis
(a)	Human rights mainstreaming – Objective

24.19	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the promotion and protection of all human rights through strengthened integration of all human rights into areas of work of the United Nations system.
Strategy
24.20	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Lead cooperation within the United Nations system for the integration of human rights into United Nations programmes and activities;

(b)	Promote the practical integration of human rights into development programmes, ensuring consistency and mutual reinforcement between United Nations-supported development activities and country engagement strategies that are jointly agreed upon with the Member States;

(c)	Contribute to developing the capability and knowledge of the United Nations system regarding relevant human rights issues through training and advice, taking into account disability and mainstreaming a gender perspective;

(d)	Develop methodological tools to facilitate the practical integration of human rights into United Nations policies, programmes and activities and, where appropriate, the advancement of the practical application of approaches to that end.
24.21	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	The United Nations system further integrating human rights into its respective programmes and activities;
(b)	Member States, pursuant to their request for support, building and strengthening national human rights promotion and protection capacities.
24.34	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will pursue a multidimensional strategy. The subprogramme will:

(a)	Enhance awareness, knowledge and understanding about the content and importance of the right to development and identify obstacles at the international, regional and national levels, through increased engagement, research, advocacy and information and educational activities, with particular focus on least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States;

(b)	Promote the realization of the right to development across the human rights programme and encourage relevant bodies of the United Nations system, as well as funds and specialized agencies, to integrate the right to development into their work and increase focus on its practical implementation;

(c)	Strengthen partnerships and collaboration with Member States, development agencies and international development, financial and trade institutions and civil society, including NGOs and the private sector;

(d)	Encourage the promotion of the right to development in global development partnerships for the implementation by Member States of the 2030 Agenda, through cooperation and collaboration, advocacy, networking and technical advice and the establishment of partnerships, and in keeping with existing mandates;

(e)	Provide substantive support to the Human Rights Council and its subsidiary mechanisms related to the right to development, including the Intergovernmental Working Group on the Right to Development, the Expert Mechanism on the Right to Development and the Human Rights Council biennial panel on the right to development;

(f)	Provide requesting Member States with technical assistance to formulate national development strategies, such as poverty reduction papers, Sustainable Development Goals strategies and the United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks, taking into account the full respect of all human rights.

24.35	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in the integration of the promotion and protection of the right to development in global partnerships for development and policies and operational activities of relevant actors at all levels.
24.47	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Enhance the research and analysis of human rights issues, as well as the development and application of expertise in different areas, including, but not limited to, areas such as the elimination of all forms of discrimination; the rule of law, democracy and good governance; the integration of human rights into counter-terrorism and anti-trafficking measures; the implementation of the Sustainable Development Goals and poverty reduction strategies and programmes; and the protection of human rights in the context of the fight against terrorism and business activities, including in the areas of new and emerging digital technologies;

(b)	Advocate for the indivisibility, interdependence and interrelatedness of all human rights, through research, policy-oriented analysis, support to standard-setting, advocacy, the compilation of best practices and improved knowledge management and knowledge-sharing; the provision of specialized human rights services; the development of methodologies, operational guidelines and tools; capacity-building and international cooperation; and the development and delivery of human rights training, for example, within the framework of the World Programme for Human Rights Education and the Global Digital Compact;

(c)	Further develop and strengthen substantive and methodological human rights expertise to support effective engagement with countries and with global and national-level partnerships, and lead efforts within the United Nations system to meet current human rights challenges;

(d)	Provide advice and assistance to requesting Member States, the United Nations system, civil society, the media and national human rights institutions in their efforts to overcome human rights implementation gaps, promote the participation and protection of rights holders, through technical cooperation, advocacy, training, policy development, analysis and advice;

(e)	Follow up and implement the Durban Declaration and Programme of Action, the outcome document of the Durban Review Conference and related declarations and the programme of activities for the implementation of the Second International Decade for People of African Descent (2025–2034), in accordance with the Proclamation of the Second International Decade for People of African Descent (Assembly resolution 79/193).
24.48	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in enhanced knowledge, awareness and understanding of human rights issues.
Programme performance in 2025
Integration of human rights norms and standards into common country analyses and United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks

24.22	In the Pact for the Future (General Assembly resolution 79/1), it was decided that the promotion and protection of human rights and the implementation of the 2030 Agenda would be recognized as interrelated and mutually reinforcing, with the recognition that the 2030 Agenda contained a pledge to leave no one behind and envisaged a world of universal respect for and promotion of human rights and human dignity, the rule of law, justice, equality and non-discrimination. To support this decision and Assembly resolution 79/226 on the quadrennial comprehensive policy review of operational activities for development of the United Nations system, the subprogramme provides system-wide leadership for the integration of human rights norms and standards into common country analyses and United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks. A comparison of inter-agency reviews undertaken in 2022 and 2025 showed clear improvements in integrating human rights into United Nations analyses and planning, with 76.8 per cent of common country analysing who is left behind, up from 55 per cent in 2022, and 50 per cent of common country analyses reflecting recommendations from United Nations human rights mechanisms, up from 32 per cent. United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Framework programming that focused on reducing inequalities and discrimination grew from 50 to 62.7 per cent and programming that measurably contributed to implementing recommendations from United Nations human rights mechanisms increased from 27 to 43.4 per cent.

24.23	The subprogramme invested in joint guidance, practical tools and capacity-building and provided support through the provision of technical advice at the regional level, helping to ensure that economic policies supported the full realization of all human rights.

24.36	The subprogramme undertakes activities to advance the right to development, including awareness-raising, research, advocacy, capacity-building and technical assistance and support for the United Nations system. This work continues to require greater knowledge, understanding, acceptance and operationalization of the right to development at all levels (international, regional, national and local). In 2025, the subprogramme continued to seek to increase access for Member States and other stakeholders to capacity-building programmes and forums to learn and exchange theoretical knowledge, practical experiences and applied skills about the promotion and protection of the right to development.

Member States promote laws, policies and practices related to drugs that comply with international human rights law

24.49	In its resolution 79/191, the General Assembly reaffirmed its commitment to respecting, protecting and promoting all human rights, fundamental freedoms and the inherent dignity of all individuals and the rule of law in the development and implementation of drug policies. Building on this, the subprogramme supported Member States and other stakeholders in promoting laws, policies and practices related to drugs that comply with international human rights law.

24.50	The subprogramme focused its support to Member States on thematic areas, including decriminalization of the possession of drugs for personal use, the abolition of the use of the death penalty for drug-related crimes, a reduction in the use of incarceration for drug-related crimes, the promotion of treatment and harm reduction, the promotion of racial equality in drug policy, the promotion of the rights of Indigenous Peoples and encouragement of civil society participation in the formulation of drug policy.

children.text: Result 3: Member States adopt counter-terrorism laws and implement measures that are consistent with and are applied in a manner that fully respects the rights enshrined in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and their obligations under international human rights law – Proposed programme plan for 2027

24.29	To advance full compliance with Member States’ obligations under international law while countering terrorism, in particular international human rights law, the subprogramme has developed tools and guidance to support the integration of human rights norms and standards into counter‑terrorism laws and implementing measures, to help to ensure respect for principles of legal certainty and legality and to ensure that counter-terrorism laws and measures do not hinder the work and safety of civil society organizations.
Lessons learned and planned change

24.30	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the utility of mandated reports, including reports of the Special Rapporteur on the promotion and protection of human rights and fundamental freedoms while countering terrorism, could be complemented by concise checklists, operational tools, detailed guidance and targeted capacity-building that can be applied directly by policymakers and legislators, and that doing so would support report implementation. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will focus on producing and disseminating practical tools and conducting in-person training. For example, the subprogramme will provide tailored training for law enforcement officials, parliamentarians and members of the judiciary, delivered in collaboration with regional and country offices and other United Nations counter‑terrorism entities.

children.text: Result 3: advancement of the promotion of the right to development at the regional and national levels – Proposed programme plan for 2027

24.42	The Human Rights Council, in its resolution 60/7, requested the Office to strengthen its work on the promotion of the right to development at the regional level and enhance its dedicated capacity at the regional level. The subprogramme has made efforts to include the right to development in international frameworks that are necessary references in regional and national frameworks. The subprogramme has also made efforts to further advance the right to development regionally through training and practical guidance tools, regional mapping on the implementation of the right to development, and outreach, advocacy and expert-led conferences.
Lessons learned and planned change

24.43	The lesson for the subprogramme was that mainstreaming the right to development into international frameworks that are implemented at the regional and national levels would help to strengthen regional operationalization of this right. Moreover, a multifaceted and tailored approach to providing regional support through the provision of targeted guidance would help to operationalize the right to development. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will continue to deploy efforts to mainstream the right to development into international frameworks while also seeking opportunities to engage with regional and national stakeholders to address specific challenges and enhance the practical application of the right to development at the regional and national levels, including mainstreaming the right to development into relevant regional and national development frameworks. The subprogramme will also promote the identification and sharing of lessons learned and practical experiences in the operationalization of the right to development, working closely with regional and national actors.

children.text: Result 3: Member States adopt and implement evidence-based anti-racism policies and action that strengthen equality, non-discrimination, tolerance and the enjoyment of all human rights – Proposed programme plan for 2027

24.56	Anti-racism policies and action have the potential to strengthen equality, non-discrimination, tolerance and the enjoyment of all human rights by all. The subprogramme contributed to ensuring that such policies and action are evidence-based and foster positive change by increasing understanding of experiences of racial discrimination and stimulating action to prevent it. In particular, the subprogramme developed several tools and guidance materials to support the development of comprehensive anti-discrimination laws and national action plans against racism and the collection and use of disaggregated data, and to support the meaningful, inclusive and safe participation of those affected by racial discrimination.
Lessons learned and planned change

24.57	The lesson for the subprogramme was that support to Member States could be enhanced through increased engagement at the regional and national levels and through the increased participation of civil society organizations to advocate for and support the adoption and implementation of policies and action grounded in lived experiences. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will leverage its anti-racism regional advisers and strengthen support to Member States in various regions.

children.text: (b)	Right to development – Objective

24.33	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the promotion and protection of the effective enjoyment by all of all human rights through the effective realization of the right to development.
Strategy
24.20	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Lead cooperation within the United Nations system for the integration of human rights into United Nations programmes and activities;

(b)	Promote the practical integration of human rights into development programmes, ensuring consistency and mutual reinforcement between United Nations-supported development activities and country engagement strategies that are jointly agreed upon with the Member States;

(c)	Contribute to developing the capability and knowledge of the United Nations system regarding relevant human rights issues through training and advice, taking into account disability and mainstreaming a gender perspective;

(d)	Develop methodological tools to facilitate the practical integration of human rights into United Nations policies, programmes and activities and, where appropriate, the advancement of the practical application of approaches to that end.
24.21	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	The United Nations system further integrating human rights into its respective programmes and activities;
(b)	Member States, pursuant to their request for support, building and strengthening national human rights promotion and protection capacities.
24.34	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will pursue a multidimensional strategy. The subprogramme will:

(a)	Enhance awareness, knowledge and understanding about the content and importance of the right to development and identify obstacles at the international, regional and national levels, through increased engagement, research, advocacy and information and educational activities, with particular focus on least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States;

(b)	Promote the realization of the right to development across the human rights programme and encourage relevant bodies of the United Nations system, as well as funds and specialized agencies, to integrate the right to development into their work and increase focus on its practical implementation;

(c)	Strengthen partnerships and collaboration with Member States, development agencies and international development, financial and trade institutions and civil society, including NGOs and the private sector;

(d)	Encourage the promotion of the right to development in global development partnerships for the implementation by Member States of the 2030 Agenda, through cooperation and collaboration, advocacy, networking and technical advice and the establishment of partnerships, and in keeping with existing mandates;

(e)	Provide substantive support to the Human Rights Council and its subsidiary mechanisms related to the right to development, including the Intergovernmental Working Group on the Right to Development, the Expert Mechanism on the Right to Development and the Human Rights Council biennial panel on the right to development;

(f)	Provide requesting Member States with technical assistance to formulate national development strategies, such as poverty reduction papers, Sustainable Development Goals strategies and the United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks, taking into account the full respect of all human rights.

24.35	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in the integration of the promotion and protection of the right to development in global partnerships for development and policies and operational activities of relevant actors at all levels.
24.47	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Enhance the research and analysis of human rights issues, as well as the development and application of expertise in different areas, including, but not limited to, areas such as the elimination of all forms of discrimination; the rule of law, democracy and good governance; the integration of human rights into counter-terrorism and anti-trafficking measures; the implementation of the Sustainable Development Goals and poverty reduction strategies and programmes; and the protection of human rights in the context of the fight against terrorism and business activities, including in the areas of new and emerging digital technologies;

(b)	Advocate for the indivisibility, interdependence and interrelatedness of all human rights, through research, policy-oriented analysis, support to standard-setting, advocacy, the compilation of best practices and improved knowledge management and knowledge-sharing; the provision of specialized human rights services; the development of methodologies, operational guidelines and tools; capacity-building and international cooperation; and the development and delivery of human rights training, for example, within the framework of the World Programme for Human Rights Education and the Global Digital Compact;

(c)	Further develop and strengthen substantive and methodological human rights expertise to support effective engagement with countries and with global and national-level partnerships, and lead efforts within the United Nations system to meet current human rights challenges;

(d)	Provide advice and assistance to requesting Member States, the United Nations system, civil society, the media and national human rights institutions in their efforts to overcome human rights implementation gaps, promote the participation and protection of rights holders, through technical cooperation, advocacy, training, policy development, analysis and advice;

(e)	Follow up and implement the Durban Declaration and Programme of Action, the outcome document of the Durban Review Conference and related declarations and the programme of activities for the implementation of the Second International Decade for People of African Descent (2025–2034), in accordance with the Proclamation of the Second International Decade for People of African Descent (Assembly resolution 79/193).
24.48	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in enhanced knowledge, awareness and understanding of human rights issues.
Programme performance in 2025
Integration of human rights norms and standards into common country analyses and United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks

24.22	In the Pact for the Future (General Assembly resolution 79/1), it was decided that the promotion and protection of human rights and the implementation of the 2030 Agenda would be recognized as interrelated and mutually reinforcing, with the recognition that the 2030 Agenda contained a pledge to leave no one behind and envisaged a world of universal respect for and promotion of human rights and human dignity, the rule of law, justice, equality and non-discrimination. To support this decision and Assembly resolution 79/226 on the quadrennial comprehensive policy review of operational activities for development of the United Nations system, the subprogramme provides system-wide leadership for the integration of human rights norms and standards into common country analyses and United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks. A comparison of inter-agency reviews undertaken in 2022 and 2025 showed clear improvements in integrating human rights into United Nations analyses and planning, with 76.8 per cent of common country analysing who is left behind, up from 55 per cent in 2022, and 50 per cent of common country analyses reflecting recommendations from United Nations human rights mechanisms, up from 32 per cent. United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Framework programming that focused on reducing inequalities and discrimination grew from 50 to 62.7 per cent and programming that measurably contributed to implementing recommendations from United Nations human rights mechanisms increased from 27 to 43.4 per cent.

24.23	The subprogramme invested in joint guidance, practical tools and capacity-building and provided support through the provision of technical advice at the regional level, helping to ensure that economic policies supported the full realization of all human rights.

children.text: Member States and other stakeholders equipped to operationalize the right to development in the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals

24.36	The subprogramme undertakes activities to advance the right to development, including awareness-raising, research, advocacy, capacity-building and technical assistance and support for the United Nations system. This work continues to require greater knowledge, understanding, acceptance and operationalization of the right to development at all levels (international, regional, national and local). In 2025, the subprogramme continued to seek to increase access for Member States and other stakeholders to capacity-building programmes and forums to learn and exchange theoretical knowledge, practical experiences and applied skills about the promotion and protection of the right to development.

Member States promote laws, policies and practices related to drugs that comply with international human rights law

24.49	In its resolution 79/191, the General Assembly reaffirmed its commitment to respecting, protecting and promoting all human rights, fundamental freedoms and the inherent dignity of all individuals and the rule of law in the development and implementation of drug policies. Building on this, the subprogramme supported Member States and other stakeholders in promoting laws, policies and practices related to drugs that comply with international human rights law.

24.50	The subprogramme focused its support to Member States on thematic areas, including decriminalization of the possession of drugs for personal use, the abolition of the use of the death penalty for drug-related crimes, a reduction in the use of incarceration for drug-related crimes, the promotion of treatment and harm reduction, the promotion of racial equality in drug policy, the promotion of the rights of Indigenous Peoples and encouragement of civil society participation in the formulation of drug policy.

children.text: Result 3: Member States adopt counter-terrorism laws and implement measures that are consistent with and are applied in a manner that fully respects the rights enshrined in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and their obligations under international human rights law – Proposed programme plan for 2027

24.29	To advance full compliance with Member States’ obligations under international law while countering terrorism, in particular international human rights law, the subprogramme has developed tools and guidance to support the integration of human rights norms and standards into counter‑terrorism laws and implementing measures, to help to ensure respect for principles of legal certainty and legality and to ensure that counter-terrorism laws and measures do not hinder the work and safety of civil society organizations.
Lessons learned and planned change

24.30	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the utility of mandated reports, including reports of the Special Rapporteur on the promotion and protection of human rights and fundamental freedoms while countering terrorism, could be complemented by concise checklists, operational tools, detailed guidance and targeted capacity-building that can be applied directly by policymakers and legislators, and that doing so would support report implementation. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will focus on producing and disseminating practical tools and conducting in-person training. For example, the subprogramme will provide tailored training for law enforcement officials, parliamentarians and members of the judiciary, delivered in collaboration with regional and country offices and other United Nations counter‑terrorism entities.

children.text: Result 3: advancement of the promotion of the right to development at the regional and national levels – Proposed programme plan for 2027

24.42	The Human Rights Council, in its resolution 60/7, requested the Office to strengthen its work on the promotion of the right to development at the regional level and enhance its dedicated capacity at the regional level. The subprogramme has made efforts to include the right to development in international frameworks that are necessary references in regional and national frameworks. The subprogramme has also made efforts to further advance the right to development regionally through training and practical guidance tools, regional mapping on the implementation of the right to development, and outreach, advocacy and expert-led conferences.
Lessons learned and planned change

24.43	The lesson for the subprogramme was that mainstreaming the right to development into international frameworks that are implemented at the regional and national levels would help to strengthen regional operationalization of this right. Moreover, a multifaceted and tailored approach to providing regional support through the provision of targeted guidance would help to operationalize the right to development. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will continue to deploy efforts to mainstream the right to development into international frameworks while also seeking opportunities to engage with regional and national stakeholders to address specific challenges and enhance the practical application of the right to development at the regional and national levels, including mainstreaming the right to development into relevant regional and national development frameworks. The subprogramme will also promote the identification and sharing of lessons learned and practical experiences in the operationalization of the right to development, working closely with regional and national actors.

children.text: Result 3: Member States adopt and implement evidence-based anti-racism policies and action that strengthen equality, non-discrimination, tolerance and the enjoyment of all human rights – Proposed programme plan for 2027

24.56	Anti-racism policies and action have the potential to strengthen equality, non-discrimination, tolerance and the enjoyment of all human rights by all. The subprogramme contributed to ensuring that such policies and action are evidence-based and foster positive change by increasing understanding of experiences of racial discrimination and stimulating action to prevent it. In particular, the subprogramme developed several tools and guidance materials to support the development of comprehensive anti-discrimination laws and national action plans against racism and the collection and use of disaggregated data, and to support the meaningful, inclusive and safe participation of those affected by racial discrimination.
Lessons learned and planned change

24.57	The lesson for the subprogramme was that support to Member States could be enhanced through increased engagement at the regional and national levels and through the increased participation of civil society organizations to advocate for and support the adoption and implementation of policies and action grounded in lived experiences. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will leverage its anti-racism regional advisers and strengthen support to Member States in various regions.

children.text: (c)	Research and analysis – Objective

24.46	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the promotion and protection of the effective enjoyment by all of all human rights through increased knowledge, awareness and understanding.
Strategy
24.20	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Lead cooperation within the United Nations system for the integration of human rights into United Nations programmes and activities;

(b)	Promote the practical integration of human rights into development programmes, ensuring consistency and mutual reinforcement between United Nations-supported development activities and country engagement strategies that are jointly agreed upon with the Member States;

(c)	Contribute to developing the capability and knowledge of the United Nations system regarding relevant human rights issues through training and advice, taking into account disability and mainstreaming a gender perspective;

(d)	Develop methodological tools to facilitate the practical integration of human rights into United Nations policies, programmes and activities and, where appropriate, the advancement of the practical application of approaches to that end.
24.21	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	The United Nations system further integrating human rights into its respective programmes and activities;
(b)	Member States, pursuant to their request for support, building and strengthening national human rights promotion and protection capacities.
24.34	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will pursue a multidimensional strategy. The subprogramme will:

(a)	Enhance awareness, knowledge and understanding about the content and importance of the right to development and identify obstacles at the international, regional and national levels, through increased engagement, research, advocacy and information and educational activities, with particular focus on least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States;

(b)	Promote the realization of the right to development across the human rights programme and encourage relevant bodies of the United Nations system, as well as funds and specialized agencies, to integrate the right to development into their work and increase focus on its practical implementation;

(c)	Strengthen partnerships and collaboration with Member States, development agencies and international development, financial and trade institutions and civil society, including NGOs and the private sector;

(d)	Encourage the promotion of the right to development in global development partnerships for the implementation by Member States of the 2030 Agenda, through cooperation and collaboration, advocacy, networking and technical advice and the establishment of partnerships, and in keeping with existing mandates;

(e)	Provide substantive support to the Human Rights Council and its subsidiary mechanisms related to the right to development, including the Intergovernmental Working Group on the Right to Development, the Expert Mechanism on the Right to Development and the Human Rights Council biennial panel on the right to development;

(f)	Provide requesting Member States with technical assistance to formulate national development strategies, such as poverty reduction papers, Sustainable Development Goals strategies and the United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks, taking into account the full respect of all human rights.

24.35	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in the integration of the promotion and protection of the right to development in global partnerships for development and policies and operational activities of relevant actors at all levels.
24.47	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Enhance the research and analysis of human rights issues, as well as the development and application of expertise in different areas, including, but not limited to, areas such as the elimination of all forms of discrimination; the rule of law, democracy and good governance; the integration of human rights into counter-terrorism and anti-trafficking measures; the implementation of the Sustainable Development Goals and poverty reduction strategies and programmes; and the protection of human rights in the context of the fight against terrorism and business activities, including in the areas of new and emerging digital technologies;

(b)	Advocate for the indivisibility, interdependence and interrelatedness of all human rights, through research, policy-oriented analysis, support to standard-setting, advocacy, the compilation of best practices and improved knowledge management and knowledge-sharing; the provision of specialized human rights services; the development of methodologies, operational guidelines and tools; capacity-building and international cooperation; and the development and delivery of human rights training, for example, within the framework of the World Programme for Human Rights Education and the Global Digital Compact;

(c)	Further develop and strengthen substantive and methodological human rights expertise to support effective engagement with countries and with global and national-level partnerships, and lead efforts within the United Nations system to meet current human rights challenges;

(d)	Provide advice and assistance to requesting Member States, the United Nations system, civil society, the media and national human rights institutions in their efforts to overcome human rights implementation gaps, promote the participation and protection of rights holders, through technical cooperation, advocacy, training, policy development, analysis and advice;

(e)	Follow up and implement the Durban Declaration and Programme of Action, the outcome document of the Durban Review Conference and related declarations and the programme of activities for the implementation of the Second International Decade for People of African Descent (2025–2034), in accordance with the Proclamation of the Second International Decade for People of African Descent (Assembly resolution 79/193).
24.48	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in enhanced knowledge, awareness and understanding of human rights issues.
Programme performance in 2025
Integration of human rights norms and standards into common country analyses and United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks

24.22	In the Pact for the Future (General Assembly resolution 79/1), it was decided that the promotion and protection of human rights and the implementation of the 2030 Agenda would be recognized as interrelated and mutually reinforcing, with the recognition that the 2030 Agenda contained a pledge to leave no one behind and envisaged a world of universal respect for and promotion of human rights and human dignity, the rule of law, justice, equality and non-discrimination. To support this decision and Assembly resolution 79/226 on the quadrennial comprehensive policy review of operational activities for development of the United Nations system, the subprogramme provides system-wide leadership for the integration of human rights norms and standards into common country analyses and United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks. A comparison of inter-agency reviews undertaken in 2022 and 2025 showed clear improvements in integrating human rights into United Nations analyses and planning, with 76.8 per cent of common country analysing who is left behind, up from 55 per cent in 2022, and 50 per cent of common country analyses reflecting recommendations from United Nations human rights mechanisms, up from 32 per cent. United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Framework programming that focused on reducing inequalities and discrimination grew from 50 to 62.7 per cent and programming that measurably contributed to implementing recommendations from United Nations human rights mechanisms increased from 27 to 43.4 per cent.

24.23	The subprogramme invested in joint guidance, practical tools and capacity-building and provided support through the provision of technical advice at the regional level, helping to ensure that economic policies supported the full realization of all human rights.

children.text: Member States and other stakeholders equipped to operationalize the right to development in the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals

24.36	The subprogramme undertakes activities to advance the right to development, including awareness-raising, research, advocacy, capacity-building and technical assistance and support for the United Nations system. This work continues to require greater knowledge, understanding, acceptance and operationalization of the right to development at all levels (international, regional, national and local). In 2025, the subprogramme continued to seek to increase access for Member States and other stakeholders to capacity-building programmes and forums to learn and exchange theoretical knowledge, practical experiences and applied skills about the promotion and protection of the right to development.

Member States promote laws, policies and practices related to drugs that comply with international human rights law

24.49	In its resolution 79/191, the General Assembly reaffirmed its commitment to respecting, protecting and promoting all human rights, fundamental freedoms and the inherent dignity of all individuals and the rule of law in the development and implementation of drug policies. Building on this, the subprogramme supported Member States and other stakeholders in promoting laws, policies and practices related to drugs that comply with international human rights law.

24.50	The subprogramme focused its support to Member States on thematic areas, including decriminalization of the possession of drugs for personal use, the abolition of the use of the death penalty for drug-related crimes, a reduction in the use of incarceration for drug-related crimes, the promotion of treatment and harm reduction, the promotion of racial equality in drug policy, the promotion of the rights of Indigenous Peoples and encouragement of civil society participation in the formulation of drug policy.

children.text: Result 3: Member States adopt counter-terrorism laws and implement measures that are consistent with and are applied in a manner that fully respects the rights enshrined in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and their obligations under international human rights law – Proposed programme plan for 2027

24.29	To advance full compliance with Member States’ obligations under international law while countering terrorism, in particular international human rights law, the subprogramme has developed tools and guidance to support the integration of human rights norms and standards into counter‑terrorism laws and implementing measures, to help to ensure respect for principles of legal certainty and legality and to ensure that counter-terrorism laws and measures do not hinder the work and safety of civil society organizations.
Lessons learned and planned change

24.30	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the utility of mandated reports, including reports of the Special Rapporteur on the promotion and protection of human rights and fundamental freedoms while countering terrorism, could be complemented by concise checklists, operational tools, detailed guidance and targeted capacity-building that can be applied directly by policymakers and legislators, and that doing so would support report implementation. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will focus on producing and disseminating practical tools and conducting in-person training. For example, the subprogramme will provide tailored training for law enforcement officials, parliamentarians and members of the judiciary, delivered in collaboration with regional and country offices and other United Nations counter‑terrorism entities.

children.text: Result 3: advancement of the promotion of the right to development at the regional and national levels – Proposed programme plan for 2027

24.42	The Human Rights Council, in its resolution 60/7, requested the Office to strengthen its work on the promotion of the right to development at the regional level and enhance its dedicated capacity at the regional level. The subprogramme has made efforts to include the right to development in international frameworks that are necessary references in regional and national frameworks. The subprogramme has also made efforts to further advance the right to development regionally through training and practical guidance tools, regional mapping on the implementation of the right to development, and outreach, advocacy and expert-led conferences.
Lessons learned and planned change

24.43	The lesson for the subprogramme was that mainstreaming the right to development into international frameworks that are implemented at the regional and national levels would help to strengthen regional operationalization of this right. Moreover, a multifaceted and tailored approach to providing regional support through the provision of targeted guidance would help to operationalize the right to development. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will continue to deploy efforts to mainstream the right to development into international frameworks while also seeking opportunities to engage with regional and national stakeholders to address specific challenges and enhance the practical application of the right to development at the regional and national levels, including mainstreaming the right to development into relevant regional and national development frameworks. The subprogramme will also promote the identification and sharing of lessons learned and practical experiences in the operationalization of the right to development, working closely with regional and national actors.

children.text: Result 3: Member States adopt and implement evidence-based anti-racism policies and action that strengthen equality, non-discrimination, tolerance and the enjoyment of all human rights – Proposed programme plan for 2027

24.56	Anti-racism policies and action have the potential to strengthen equality, non-discrimination, tolerance and the enjoyment of all human rights by all. The subprogramme contributed to ensuring that such policies and action are evidence-based and foster positive change by increasing understanding of experiences of racial discrimination and stimulating action to prevent it. In particular, the subprogramme developed several tools and guidance materials to support the development of comprehensive anti-discrimination laws and national action plans against racism and the collection and use of disaggregated data, and to support the meaningful, inclusive and safe participation of those affected by racial discrimination.
Lessons learned and planned change

24.57	The lesson for the subprogramme was that support to Member States could be enhanced through increased engagement at the regional and national levels and through the increased participation of civil society organizations to advocate for and support the adoption and implementation of policies and action grounded in lived experiences. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will leverage its anti-racism regional advisers and strengthen support to Member States in various regions.

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Supporting human rights treaty bodies
Objective

24.60	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the promotion and protection of the effective enjoyment of all human rights by all, in particular through support and advice to the human rights treaty bodies and by increasing awareness among national and international actors of the international human rights treaties and the work of treaty bodies.
Strategy
24.61	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide support for the review of Member State reports and the preparation of general comments; for the conduct of inquiries and country visits, including to places of deprivation of liberty where provided for in the treaty or upon request of Member States; for early warning and urgent action procedures and the processing of individual complaints, urgent actions and inter-State complaints; as well as assistance and advice to the national preventive mechanisms;
(b)	Support the efforts of treaty bodies to improve and enhance their working methods;

(c)	Engage with Member States, national human rights institutions, United Nations agencies, NGOs and other actors to enhance awareness, knowledge and understanding of the treaty bodies’ work and outputs and to promote ratification of international human rights treaties;

(d)	Support States Parties, upon request, in building their capacity to implement their treaty obligations and in the preparation and timely submission of their national reports, as well as in in establishing or strengthening national mechanisms for reporting and follow-up through the exchange of experiences and good practices;

(e)	Assist treaty bodies in their efforts to address reprisals when they occur, and in their responses to claims and acts of intimidation or reprisals against individuals and groups for their contribution to the work of the human rights treaty bodies.
24.62	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Efficient and effective functioning of the treaty bodies;
(b)	Enhanced cooperation of stakeholders at all levels with treaty bodies in accordance with their working methods and mandates.
Programme performance in 2025
Increased adoption by the treaty bodies of lists of issues and lists of issues prior to reporting

24.63	In response to the cancellation of meetings and reduced meeting times, the Chairs of treaty bodies, at their meeting in June, recommended that the treaty bodies adopt exceptional measures to be able to implement their mandates during 2025. The three committees whose pre-sessional working groups had been cancelled adopted preparatory documentation for the State Party reviews (list of issues and list of issues prior to reporting) online or through the silence procedure to ensure continuity of the work. The subprogramme facilitated this exceptional working method by advising treaty bodies on the application of the rules of procedure and working methods for online consideration, decision-making and the adoption of lists of issues and lists of issues prior to reporting.
24.64	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 24.III).
Figure 24.III
Performance measure: number of lists of issues and lists of issues prior to reporting adopted by the treaty bodies (annual)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: increased international cooperation to support national mechanisms for implementation, reporting and follow-up – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

24.65	The subprogramme’s work contributed to increased international cooperation to support national mechanisms for implementation, reporting and follow-up, with 47 countries having shared practices thereon through the national mechanisms for implementation, reporting and follow-up knowledge hub, established in 2025 pursuant to Human Rights Council resolution 51/33, which exceeded the planned target of 20.
24.66	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 24.IV).
Figure 24.IV
Performance measure: number of countries that have shared practices on implementation, reporting and follow up to the international human rights mechanisms through the knowledge hub (cumulative)
Result 2: improved access by State Parties and stakeholders to the recommendations of treaty bodies to facilitate their implementation – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

24.67	The subprogramme’s work contributed to access to the treaty body database by 548,454 users, including Member States, rights holders and other stakeholders, which did not meet the planned target of 550,000. The target was not met owing to the late confirmation and cancellation of treaty bodies’ meetings and visits. That unpredictability resulted in the reduced engagement by the targeted users.
24.68	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 24.V).
Figure 24.V
Performance measure: number of users of the treaty body database (annual)
Result 3: one-stop shop for gaining access to information on the work of treaty bodies – Proposed programme plan for 2027

24.69	Access to information on the work of treaty bodies supports the engagement by States and other stakeholders in these processes. To date, the subprogramme has established connections between platforms, such as integrating the calendars of sessions of treaty bodies and the universal periodic review, thereby providing an overview of countries scheduled to be reviewed by these human rights mechanisms. However, this still requires users to consult multiple sources to locate the information that they need.
Lessons learned and planned change

24.70	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the provision of information, such as reporting records and deadlines, across multiple platforms created challenges in gaining access to information on engagement with treaty bodies, across multiple platforms, which has affected the ability of States to meet their reporting obligations and of stakeholders to plan their engagement. The subprogramme plans to develop a one-stop platform for information related to treaty bodies.

Subprogramme 3 – Advisory services, technical cooperation and field activities
Objective

24.73	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the promotion and protection of the effective enjoyment by all of all human rights, through enhanced capacity-building, including through assistance to requesting Member States.
Strategy
24.74	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide Member States, upon their request, and stakeholders at the national and regional levels with human rights legal advice, education, awareness-raising and training through mutually agreed advisory services and technical cooperation programmes, including in the implementation of the recommendations to which Member States have agreed in the universal periodic review process;

(b)	Improve cooperation within the United Nations system for the protection and promotion of human rights, through joint activities, the deployment of human rights officers, interaction with the Office’s programmes in the field and relevant United Nations human rights mechanisms;

(c)	Continue to deploy human rights experts and fact-finding missions, including in humanitarian emergencies, at the request of an affected country or as mandated by the relevant United Nations legislative bodies;

(d)	Engage with Member States, United Nations system entities and other relevant stakeholders to implement an integrated system-wide response with regard to the protection of victims’ rights and assistance to victims of sexual exploitation and abuse by United Nations personnel, uniformed or civilian, in peacekeeping, humanitarian and development settings and by non-United Nations personnel serving under a Security Council mandate, and provide guidance to the Senior Victims’ Rights Officers, the victims’ rights focal points and other actors in the field in this regard;

(e)	Assist the Human Rights Council and its mechanisms, as well as other policymaking bodies and the treaty bodies, in preparing for and following up on their dialogue with Member States and ensure the efficient and effective functioning of thematic and country-specific special procedures by following human rights developments.
24.75	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced national capacities to translate international human rights obligations into effective laws, regulations and policies and meet the challenges to the full realization of human rights;

(b)	Enhanced capacity of United Nations country teams, field presences, peacekeeping operations and peacebuilding activities to assist requesting countries in their efforts to develop national human rights protection systems, guided, inter alia, by the recommendations of the international human rights mechanisms;

(c)	Stronger system-wide collaboration and cooperation between Member States and the United Nations on accountability processes for sexual exploitation and abuse by United Nations personnel and non-United Nations personnel serving under a Security Council mandate, and on restorative measures and access to services for victims;
(d)	Prevention of human rights violations, including in situations of large-scale crisis.

Programme performance in 2025 – Human Rights Council-mandated investigations contribute to accountability processes by providing comprehensive, independent and evidence-based analyses of human rights violations and abuses of international humanitarian law and international human rights law

24.76	Human Rights Council investigations are used to respond to and document serious violations of international humanitarian law and international human rights law, preserving evidence in line with international standards, and giving visibility to victims, thereby supporting pathways to justice, accountability and non‑recurrence. The subprogramme strengthened the institutional support framework for investigations by enabling faster start‑up, surge capacity, centralized services, harmonized approaches and strengthened engagement on accountability pathways. This contributed to the improved quality, consistency and reliability of investigative outputs and supported victim‑centred approaches, accountability processes and judicial cooperation. In 2025, the subprogramme delivered centralized operational, technical and surge support to 12 human rights council‑mandated investigative bodies, including the rapid operationalization of the fact‑finding mission on eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, ensuring timely mandate implementation. It provided forensic medical guidance for evidence collection, legal advice on international human rights, humanitarian and criminal law, and technical support to integrate child‑specific violations, a gender perspective and victim‑centred analysis into reporting. All Council-mandated investigative bodies were equipped with essential digital tools, secure infrastructure and specialized technical expertise to ensure the collection, preservation and analysis of digital evidence in line with international standards. The subprogramme also strengthened the evidentiary value of information collected, which improved the response to requests for assistance from judicial and legal authorities.

children.text: Result 3: Member States and other stakeholders take action to address human rights situations at the country and regional levels – Proposed programme plan for 2027

24.82	Building on recent calls for the United Nations to work collectively, joining perspectives and developing a shared analysis of geographical and thematic situations to allow for stronger decision-making within United Nations country teams and, in turn, informed action by Member States, the subprogramme works with Emergency Response Teams to produce reports and dashboards of human rights analysis and situations.
Lessons learned and planned change

24.83	The lesson for the subprogramme was that increased utilization of the Emergency Response Teams situated in regional offices could help to strengthen geographical coverage by bringing assistance closer to where situations are developing. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will generate a more holistic analysis of human rights situations, including economic and social dimensions of human rights challenges, combined with the deployment of thematic expertise to the regions. The subprogramme will build on the work of the seven Emergency Response Teams, deployed to Bangkok, Dakar, Pretoria, Yaoundé, Panama City, Santiago and Suva, to further strengthen their capacity and the provision of data-processing tools, including media monitoring and mapping services such as data visualizations, dashboards, infographics and visual snapshots for the production of dashboards, analysis of regional and country-specific emerging trends and patterns, and the identification of mitigating measures adopted by Governments in their regions.

children.text: Subprogramme 4 – Supporting the Human Rights Council, its subsidiary bodies and mechanisms
Objective

24.86	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the promotion and protection of the effective enjoyment by all of all human rights, by providing strengthened support and advice to the Human Rights Council and its subsidiary bodies and mechanisms, including the Human Rights Council Advisory Committee, the special procedures, the universal periodic review and the complaint procedure.
Strategy
24.87	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Research and develop analytical information and knowledge in support of the thematic special procedures and the universal periodic review mechanism to enhance their effectiveness;

(b)	Support and provide thematic expertise for fact-finding missions and special procedure mandate holders’ country visits, including through enhanced cooperation within the programme, analysis of gaps in the implementation of international human rights instruments, promotion of observance for international human rights standards and provision of timely advice for addressing gross and systematic violations of human rights;
(c)	Support the international human rights mechanisms in their efforts to promote the implementation of the 2030 Agenda in accordance with the human rights obligations of Member States;

(d)	Disseminate knowledge of the conclusions, recommendations and other outcomes of the universal periodic review, as well as the findings and methodology of the thematic special procedures, and improve coordination among mandate holders and other mechanisms of the human rights machinery;

(e)	Support partnerships and reinforce dialogue and cooperation with Governments, national human rights institutions, civil society organizations, United Nations system and the United Nations human rights machinery and cooperating policymaking bodies, including to support follow-up to the findings and recommendations of the special procedure mandate holders and the outcomes of the universal periodic review;
(f)	Provide assistance to Member States within the universal periodic review framework.
24.88	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced and effective functioning of the Human Rights Council and its subsidiary bodies and mechanisms, including more effective deliberations and decision-making processes;
(b)	Enhanced cooperation at all levels with stakeholders that can benefit from and/or contribute to the work of the Human Rights Council and its subsidiary bodies and mechanisms.
Programme performance in 2025
Enhanced impact of special procedures at the global, regional and national levels

24.89	Special procedures are a core mechanism of the Human Rights Council to support the promotion and protection of human rights. Their impact can be strengthened with sustained, strategic engagement with a broad range of stakeholders to translate findings and recommendations into concrete action and measurable improvements in the enjoyment of human rights. In 2025, special procedures mandate holders enhanced their cooperation with States and other stakeholders and strengthened their outreach to international and regional organizations, national human rights institutions, civil society and the private sector. Those efforts contributed to the increased visibility, impact and follow-up of recommendations at all levels.
24.90	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 24.VIII).
Figure 24.VIII
Performance measure: number of communications and number of responses from Member States and other stakeholders
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: enhanced contribution of national human rights institutions to the universal periodic review mechanism – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

24.91	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the submission of 36 written and oral contributions to the universal periodic review mechanism by national human rights institutions accredited in full compliance with the Paris Principles, which did not meet the target of 48. The target was not met owing primarily to several national human rights institutions accredited with “A” status encountering budgetary difficulties in participating in the adoption of the review reports of their countries.
24.92	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 24.IX).
Figure 24.IX
Performance measure: number of written and oral submissions made by national human rights institutions during the universal periodic review (annual)
Result 2: enhanced engagement of United Nations country teams with the universal periodic review mechanism – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

24.93	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the submission of 26 written contributions to the universal periodic review mechanism by United Nations country teams, which did not meet the target of 33. The target was not met owing primarily to several United Nations agencies opting to submit individual contributions through their headquarters instead of contributing to the country teams’ joint submissions.
24.94	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 24.X).
Figure 24.X
Performance measure: number of written submissions made by United Nations country teams during the universal periodic review (annual)
Result 3: improved access of persons with disabilities to the work of the Human Rights Council – Proposed programme plan for 2027

24.95	The subprogramme strives to improve the access of persons with disabilities to the work of the Human Rights Council. Through its sustained support for the work of the task force for accessibility for persons with disabilities, pursuant to Council resolution 16/21, the subprogramme has identified gaps and areas for improvements in the existing framework for the participation of persons with disabilities in the work of the Council. For example, the subprogramme was able to increase the accessibility of the online platforms used for inscriptions on lists of speakers for sessions of the Council, as well as for registrations to participate in the sessions.
Lessons learned and planned change

24.96	The lesson for the subprogramme was that persons with disabilities continued to face barriers to effective participation in the work of the United Nations human rights machinery. Identified barriers include limitations in physical access to Human Rights Council sessions, insufficient availability of easy-to-read versions of official documentation considered during sessions and only a limited number of Council meetings mandated to be accessible. In applying the lesson and to address these barriers, the subprogramme will strengthen its advocacy for an increase in the number of meetings mandated to be accessible during the regular sessions of the Council. This will include, among other, systematically recommending that mandated panel discussions be made accessible through the provision of live captioning and sign language interpretation. In addition, the subprogramme will collect feedback from persons with disabilities participating in Council sessions on their experience to support the collection the lessons learned to relevant United Nations counterparts to ensure that this informs decision-making on issues aiming at facilitating participation of persons with disabilities in meetings of the Council.
24.97	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 24.XI).
Figure 24.XI
Performance measure: number of meetings of the Human Rights Council accessible to persons with disabilities
